Section6.title: Our Mission
Section6.desc: MEER’s mission is to develop, deploy, and promote surface-based cooling technologies that reduce global heat stress, rebalance the planet’s energy system, and support climate justice through open, evidence-driven solutions.

Section6.title: Research
Section6.desc: We combine scientific research, engineering innovation, and community partnership to deliver real-world impact — coolingcities, protecting livelihoods, and restoring local resilience in regions most affected by extreme heat.

Section6.title: Trust & Integrity
Section6.desc: MEER is an independent, globally connected nonprofit committed to transparency, inclusivity, and measurable impact-collaborating widely without compromising research integrity or independence.

Section6.title: Our Approach
Section6.desc: Our approach focuses on practical action and open science, not ideology - working within real-world systems to drive meaningful climate solutions through ethical design, education, and community empowerment.

Section6.title: Technology
Section6.desc: Ultimately, MEER seeks to demonstrate that global cooling and local equity are achievable together-that technology, science, and collaboration can unite to rebalance the Earth’s climate system and build a more livable, just, and sustainable planet for all.

Section8.smallDescription:
In practice, this means prioritizing material efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and real-world deployability. Our currentreflective sheeting systemsare lightweight, modular,and low-cost, with material costs below $1 per square meter and installed system costs around $20 per square meter in real-world settings.

Alongside these systems, MEER is developing next-generation passive cooling coatings based on passive daytime radiative cooling (PDRC) principles. These coatings are designed not only to reflect incoming solar radiation but also to emit heat more effectively, improving overall cooling performance. Our current development pathway focuses on scalable, water-based formulations suitablefor low-cost deployment, with estimated costs around $5 per square meter today and a target of approximately $2 per square meter as the technology matures.

strategyData.title: Passive Technology
strategyData.desc: Our reflectors work without electricity once installed, providing cooling that is reliable and low-maintenance.

strategyData.title: Sustainable Materials
strategyData.desc: We design reflectors from recycled aluminum, PET plastics, and bamboo, reducing waste and avoiding new extraction.

strategyData.title: Community-First
strategyData.desc: We partner directly with local communities to design, test, and implement cooling projects that meet real human needs

strategyData.title: Scalable by Design
strategyData.desc: From a single rooftop to entire neighborhoods, our technology can expand quickly and affordably.

section1.heading: Why Cooling Matters Now
section1.detail:
Extreme heat is already among the most lethal climate-related hazards globally, and its intensity is rising faster than many mitigation pathways can counteract. Even under optimistic emissions reductions, global temperatures will continue to increase for decades. This is due to thermal inertia in the oceans and the long atmospheric lifetime of existing greenhouse gases.

That means one hard truth: heat risk is already locked in.

Cooling interventions address this immediate and unavoidable danger. They reduce exposure, protect vulnerable populations, and stabilise essential systems while longer-term decarbonisation efforts scale.

Cooling is not a shortcut around emissions reductions.  It is a protective measure that reduces harm, buys time, and preserves human habitability during a period of unavoidable warming.

Cooling is not a shortcut around emissions reductions.It is a protective measure that reduces harm, buys time, and preserves human habitability during a period of unavoidable warming.

section2.heading: Ground-Based Cooling Pathways
section2.detail:
Cooling can occur through physical processes that operate directly at the Earth’s surface and within the built environment. These approaches do not rely on atmospheric manipulation or speculative technologies. They work through established principles of heat transfer, reflectivity, and material science.

  • Increasing surface reflectivity to prevent solar absorption
  • Reducing heat storage in roofs, walls, pavements, and urban materials
  • Enhancing passive cooling through design and material selection
  • Limiting indoor heat exposure without dependence on energy-intensive air conditioning

These interventions modify how heat interacts with surfaces before it becomes trapped within buildings or urban systems.

Because they operate at ground level, they are:

  • Local and measurable
  • Local and measurable
  • Reversible if needed
  • Immediately protective

They reduce heat where people actually experience it.

section3.heading: Local Benefits, Global Co-Benefits
section3.detail:
The most immediate effects of cooling interventions are human-centred and practical:

  • Lower indoor temperatures
  • Reduced heat stress and mortality risk
  • Improved productivity and comfort
  • Greater resilience in homes, schools, and workplaces
  • Reduced reliance on mechanical cooling

These are direct benefits that can be measured in degrees, health outcomes, and energy demand.

At larger scales, these local reductions generate broader systemic effects. Lower absorbed heat reduces peak electricity loads, decreases strain on energy infrastructure, and limits additional emissions from expanded air conditioning use. Over time and across large surface areas, reduced solar absorption contributes incrementally to lowering net heat accumulation in the Earth system.

These global effects are cumulative and complementary. They do not replace emissions reduction. They strengthen it by reducing parallel energy demand and heat amplification.

section4.heading: Adaptive Mitigation
section4.detail:
Conventional climate policy separates adaptation from mitigation. Adaptation helps people cope with rising temperatures. Mitigation reduces greenhouse gas emissions to limit future warming.

MEER integrates both through what we call adaptive mitigation.This approach recognises that surface-based passive cooling can protect people today while also contributing to reductions in net heat absorption.

  • Adaptation dimension:
    Cooling homes, schools, agricultural systems, and water infrastructure without electricity or refrigerants
  • Mitigation dimension:
    Increasing surface reflectivity, lowering net solar heat absorbed, and reducing pressure on energy systems.

By combining immediate protection with systemic energy balance improvement, adaptive mitigation bridges the gap between short-term survival and long-term climate stability.

Cooling is therefore not a secondary intervention. It is a stabilising layer within the broader climate response.

section5.heading: Can We Build Enough to Matter?
section5.subheading: Introducing GPTO — A Climate-Scale Capability Framework
section5.detail:
• The Missing Question
    When climate cooling solutions are evaluated, the focus is usually on performance — how well they work, how much heat they reduce, and what they cost. But there is another critical question: can we produce enough of these solutions, every year, to make a difference at planetary scale? GPTO is designed to answer that.
  • What Is GPTO?
    GPTO (Global Production Thermal Offset) measures whether global material production is sufficient to scale a cooling system to match the magnitude of the climate challenge. In simple terms, it asks whether the world’s annual supply of a material is enough to build a system capable of offsetting global warming impacts in a single year. If not, material supply becomes the limiting factor — regardless of performance.
  • Why It Matters?
    Climate solutions must operate at industrial scale. Each year, humanity emits tens of gigatons of CO₂e, and all cooling systems depend on materials already in demand across global industries. If those materials are not produced in sufficient quantities, the system cannot scale. GPTO makes this constraint visible, distinguishing between ideas that are effective and those that are truly scalable.
  • Interpreting GPTO
    GPTO produces a value that indicates scalability. Lower values suggest local or regional impact, while values near or above one indicate that annual production could support climate-scale deployment. For systems using multiple materials, the most constrained component determines overall scalability.
  • Designing for Climate Scale
    GPTO shifts how we think about climate solutions. It moves the focus beyond performance and cost toward material availability and real-world scalability. The climate crisis is measured in gigatons — and any serious solution must operate at that scale. GPTO ensures we ask the essential question early: can we build enough of this to matter?

section1.heading: A Thermodynamic Measure for Real Climate Solutions
section1.detail:
Cooling Return on Investment (CROI) is a physics-based metric developed by Dr. Ye Tao of MEER to determine whether a climate solution can genuinely cool the planet. It does not measure emissions reductions, carbon credits, or financial returns. It measures something more fundamental: how much heat is removed from the Earth system relative to the energy required to deploy and operate the solution.

Global warming is not primarily an accounting problem. It is an energy imbalance. Excess solar energy is being trapped in the Earth system, accumulating as heat in oceans, land, and atmosphere. Any intervention that claims to stabilize climate must therefore be evaluated by whether it reduces that heat burden in physical terms.

CROI provides that thermodynamic lens.

section2.heading: What CROI Measures
section2.detail:
CROI quantifies the ratio between heat removed (or prevented from entering the climate system) and the total energy invested across the lifecycle of a solution — including extraction, manufacturing, deployment, and operation.

If one unit of energy removes two units of heat, CROI = 2. If one unit removes 1,500 units of heat, CROI = 1,500. At planetary scale, only extremely high ratios matter.

CROI is analogous to Energy Return on Investment (EROI), which determines whether an energy source is viable for society. In the same way, CROI determines whether a climate intervention is viable for planetary cooling. It is not about popularity, policy alignment, or marketability. It is about thermodynamics.
